title: Mastery Exam: One-Dimensional Layout with Flexbox
summary: Mastery Exam: One-Dimensional Layout with Flexbox retrieves prior skills in an original independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision scenario and requires implementation, behavior, failure-state, and transfer evidence.
objectives: Apply flex formatting context, flex sizing algorithm, flex alignment, flex wrapping, flex order accessibility, flex pattern selection in unfamiliar constraints.
Produce or audit a real independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision artifact without happy-path-only reasoning.
Preserve earlier requirements while testing keyboard, narrow-width, invalid, and assistive-semantic states.

starterFiles.html: <!-- Continue the Community Needs Survey one-dimensional layout work here. -->
starterFiles.css: /* Continue the resilient Flexbox patterns here. */

steps.title: 01 · answer 1: survey action-row axis investigation · quiz
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim survives the changed case? Scenario: independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision.
steps.why: Mastery Exam: One-Dimensional Layout with Flexbox makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 1 in independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision.

content.title: independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision evidence
content.text: For Mastery Exam: One-Dimensional Layout with Flexbox, choose the flex formatting context claim that preserves the independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision purpose, native behavior, and understandable relationships when presentation changes.

hints: Mastery Exam: One-Dimensional Layout with Flexbox checkpoint 1: identify the user task and data relationship first.
Mastery Exam: One-Dimensional Layout with Flexbox checkpoint 1: compare source, native behavior, and announced meaning.
Mastery Exam: One-Dimensional Layout with Flexbox checkpoint 1: make the smallest repair that preserves earlier passing work.

options.text: Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 1: the main axis is always horizontal and the cross axis always vertical.
Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 1: read writing mode, direction, and flex-direction first, then name the main and cross axes before choosing sizing or alignment.
Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 1: use justify-content for every horizontal decision because horizontal always means main axis.

options.text: Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 2: flex one means each item always receives exactly the same width.
Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 2: calculate bases, container free space, grow or scaled shrink shares, and min or max clamping before predicting final sizes.
Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 2: use flex-shrink zero on every item so the container can always compress them.

options.text: independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision phase 3.1: draw main and cross axes for the current direction
independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision phase 3.2: distinguish item alignment from free-space distribution
independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision phase 3.3: choose justify content align items align content align self or gap
independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision phase 3.4: test wrapping overflow long labels and changed writing mode

options.text: Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 4: flex-wrap alone guarantees every card has a usable minimum width.

Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 4: set a content-derived flexible basis and minimum, enable wrapping, and test when real cards move to the next line across content changes.
Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 4: use order values to keep rows visually balanced after wrapping.

options.text: Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 5: changing CSS order also changes screen-reader and keyboard sequence.
Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 5: put content in logical source order and use layout that preserves it, reserving visual reordering for nonessential presentation only.
Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 5: assign order values to every form field so mobile and desktop can have unrelated sequences.

options.text: Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 6: flexbox is the modern replacement for every older layout mechanism.

Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 6: count meaningful layout dimensions, identify content or track control, and choose block flow, Flexbox, Grid, or another method from the actual relationship.
Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 6: use nested Flexbox rows to simulate every two-dimensional data grid regardless of alignment needs.

options.text: Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 9: justify-content vertically centers items in every flex container.

Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 9: use align-content on a single non-wrapping line to center its items.

options.text: Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 26: flex one means each item always receives exactly the same width.

Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 26: use flex-shrink zero on every item so the container can always compress them.

options.text: Independent layout algorithm and accessibility decision case 29: changing CSS order also changes screen-reader and keyboard sequence.
